brief: TYPE: single-patient case report. GAP/WHY: LAAC is established for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; anticoagulation-related spinal epidural hematoma (SEH) and device-related thrombosis (DRT) are rare but serious post-LAAC complications; little guidance on managing them, especially when they occur sequentially in one high-risk patient and pull antithrombotic therapy in opposite directions. DESIGN/SETTING: descriptive case report with longitudinal follow-up imaging. PATIENT: 86-year-old woman; presenting complaint = recurrent chest tightness for 6 months; diagnoses = persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation + chronic heart failure; multiple high-risk factors. INTERVENTION/COURSE: (1) underwent successful LAAC; (2) post-procedure anticoagulation = edoxaban 30 mg once daily; (3) at 6 weeks post-procedure developed anticoagulation-related SEH -> anticoagulation immediately discontinued -> conservative management -> hematoma resolved and muscle strength improved; (4) switched to antiplatelet therapy clopidogrel 75 mg once daily; (5) at 6-month follow-up, transthoracic echocardiography + CT angiography confirmed a large DRT in the left atrium; (6) anticoagulation re-initiated = low-molecular-weight heparin bridging to warfarin, INR target maintained 2.0-2.5. KEY RESULTS/OUTCOMES: follow-up imaging at 8 months showed significant resolution of the left atrial thrombus; over remainder of follow-up, no further major adverse events (no ischemic stroke, no bleeding). TAKEAWAY: in high-risk LAAC patients, antithrombotic strategy must be individualized and dynamically adjusted; emphasize perioperative procedural quality control and structured follow-up within key time windows to minimize complications and optimize net clinical benefit. NO COMPARATOR GROUP, NO p-values/CIs/effect sizes (single case).
